title: The Staff+ Tech Lead Role at FAANG
description: The Tech Lead (TL) role at FAANG is the most misunderstood Staff+ position. It is not 'the most senior engineer on the team' — it is a distinct responsibility set that sits between IC Staff Engineer and Engineering Manager. This guide covers what the TL actually does at each FAANG company, the TL pendulum (when to code vs when to lead), running design reviews at scale, the TL-PM partnership, and how SAP engineers can prepare for this role.

sections.title: The Tech Lead Role — What It Is and Isn't

description.value:
Every FAANG company has a Tech Lead role, but none of them define it the same way. The common thread: the TL is accountable for the team's technical output without having managerial authority over the people producing it. This makes the TL role fundamentally different from both Staff IC and Engineering Manager.

IC Staff Engineer: Owns deep technical problems, sets technical direction through design docs and RFCs, influences across teams. No direct reports. No team management responsibilities. Impact is through technical artifacts and individual contribution to hard problems. Key difference from TL: the Staff IC is measured on the quality of their designs and decisions. The TL is measured on the team's delivery.

Tech Lead: Owns the team's technical delivery, architecture decisions, and technical quality. Runs design reviews, sets coding standards, unblocks the team, coordinates with PMs and other TLs. No direct reports (at most companies). The TL is accountable for outcomes produced by others. Key difference from Staff IC: the TL cannot say 'I did my part, the team dropped the ball.' The TL owns the team's output.

Engineering Manager: Owns people (hiring, firing, promotions, performance reviews), team health, career growth, and delivery. Has direct reports and organizational authority. The EM sets priorities; the TL sets technical direction. Key difference from TL: the EM can say 'move to this project' and the engineer moves. The TL must persuade, negotiate, and influence.

Company-specific nuances: At Google, the TL role is formalized (Tech Lead, often paired with a TL Manager). The TL makes technical decisions; the Engineering Manager handles people. At Meta, the TL is typically the most senior IC on the team (E6+). They set direction and unblock. 'If you're the E6 on the team, you're the Tech Lead.' At Amazon, the 'principal engineer' functions as TL for a broader scope. Amazon TLs are expected to write the 6-pager for major architectural decisions. At Apple, the TL role is less formal — the 'lead' emerges based on expertise and tenure. Apple TLs are expected to know the codebase deeply and unblock by writing code.

sections.codeBlock:
# The TL Role Spectrum

# Google
TL Role: Formal (Tech Lead + TL Manager co-model)
Scope: Single team or pod
Authority: Technical decisions only
Reports: None (people mgmt is TL Manager)
Key metric: Team delivery + design quality

# Meta
TL Role: The E6+ on the team
Scope: Single team (or 2-3 small teams)
Authority: Technical direction + code review
Reports: None
Key metric: Team shipping velocity

# Amazon
TL Role: Principal engineer (L6/L7)
Scope: Organization (2-5 teams)
Authority: Architecture decisions
Reports: None
Key metric: Operational excellence + delivery

# Apple
TL Role: Emergent senior engineer
Scope: Single team
Authority: Technical expertise
Reports: None
Key metric: Code quality + unblocking

sections.title: The TL Pendulum — When to Code vs When to Lead

description.value:
The hardest decision a Tech Lead makes every day: should I be writing code or unblocking the team? The answer is 'both, but not at the same time.' The TL Pendulum is the mental model for switching between individual contribution and leadership work.

The Pendulum Model: Imagine a pendulum that swings between 'deep coding' and 'team enablement.' At any given moment, you are at one extreme or the other. You are either heads-down writing a critical migration script, or you are in 6 back-to-back sync meetings unblocking dependencies. The mistake is trying to do both simultaneously — half-finished code and half-engaged meetings produce neither.

When to swing to coding: (1) There is a P0/DSEV issue that requires your specific expertise. (2) The team is blocked on a design decision that only you can make, and implementing the prototype will validate it. (3) You are establishing credibility with a new team — writing code alongside them for the first 4-6 weeks demonstrates that you understand the system. (4) The project has a critical path item that will take 2-3 days of uninterrupted work, and no one else has the context.

When to swing to leading: (1) The team is waiting for your review to proceed. Stop coding, clear the review queue. (2) A design decision is blocked because two senior engineers disagree — your job is to facilitate the resolution, not write code. (3) The PM needs input on scope tradeoffs for the next quarter — this affects the team's work for months. (4) An engineer is stuck and has been spinning for 2+ days. A 30-minute pairing session unblocks them faster than any code you can write.

The 80/20 heuristic: Google's internal research found that the most effective Tech Leads spend roughly 80% of their time on leadership activities (design reviews, unblocking, coordination, planning) and 20% on hands-on coding. New TLs often invert this, spending 80% coding because it feels productive. The key insight: if you are coding 80% of the time, you are not leading — you are a senior IC who happens to have a TL title.

sections.codeBlock:
# TL Pendulum Decision Flow

# Ask yourself every morning:
ISSUE_1 = "Is the team blocked on something only I can unblock?"
if YES → swing to lead mode

ISSUE_2 = "Is there a production issue requiring my specific system knowledge?"
if YES → swing to coding mode

ISSUE_3 = "Is my code review queue above 5 pending reviews?"
if YES → clear queue before writing new code

ISSUE_4 = "Have I written production code in the last 2 weeks?"
if NO → schedule 4 hours of coding this week to stay grounded

# Weekly balance check:
# Monday: review-heavy, planning-heavy (lead mode)
# Tuesday-Thursday: deep work blocks (code mode, AM only)
# Friday: cleanup, reviews, documentation (lead mode)

decisionTree.prompt: Where should your pendulum swing right now?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: What is the most pressing issue facing the team today?

branches.condition: An engineer is stuck on a technical problem for 2+ days
branches.path: Swing to lead mode. Pair with them for 30 minutes to unblock. A blocked engineer costs the team $500-1000/hour in lost productivity. Your 30-minute intervention saves the team 2+ days. After unblocking, document the solution pattern so others don't get stuck on the same issue.

branches.condition: There's a cross-team dependency that isn't resolved
branches.path: Swing to lead mode. Your title and organizational capital are the tools to resolve this. Set up a sync with the other team's TL or EM. Define the contract (API, timeline, ownership). Document the agreement. This unblocks your entire team, not just one person.

branches.condition: The team shipping velocity has dropped for 2+ sprints
branches.path: Swing to lead mode. This is a systemic issue. Run a retrospective. Identify bottlenecks (review latency? dependency delays? unclear requirements?). Build a fix for the system, not a workaround for a single sprint. Your job is to make the team faster, not to write more code yourself.

branches.condition: We need a critical migration to unblock the next quarter's roadmap
branches.path: Swing to coding mode — but only for the prototype. Write the core migration script, establish the pattern, then hand off to a team member to productionize. Your job is to design and prove the approach, not to execute the full migration.

branches.condition: Performance degradation in production needs immediate investigation
branches.path: Swing to coding mode. This is a time-sensitive technical problem that may require deep system knowledge. Investigate with a team member paired alongside you so they learn the debugging approach. After fixing, write the postmortem and add the monitoring that would have caught this earlier.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The most common TL mistake is staying in coding mode too long. Code feels productive — you create something tangible. Leadership feels ambiguous — you attended meetings, unblocked people, and have nothing to show at the end of the day. Get comfortable with invisible productivity. Your impact is measured by what the team ships, not what you personally commit.

sections.title: Running Design Reviews and Setting Technical Direction

description.value:
The TL owns the team's technical direction. This does not mean the TL makes every architectural decision. It means the TL creates the system for making good decisions: design review process, technical decision records, and a framework for evaluating tradeoffs.

The design review cadence: (1) Weekly design review slot (60-90 minutes) — non-negotiable, recurring. All medium-to-large technical decisions go through this review. (2) Pre-read requirement — design docs must be shared 48 hours before the review. No pre-read, no review. This forces engineers to write down their thinking before defending it. (3) Decision log — every review ends with a written summary: decision made, alternatives considered, open questions, next steps. Published to the team's decision log. (4) Escalation path — if the review identifies a decision that affects 3+ teams, it escalates to the org-level design review.

Setting technical direction without being a dictator: The TL's goal is to create alignment, not to impose decisions. The pattern: (1) Identify the decision that needs to be made. 'We need to decide on the event format for the payment service.' (2) Define the evaluation criteria. 'We will evaluate on: schema evolution support, tooling maturity, client library availability, and performance at 10K events/second.' (3) Write the strawman proposal. 'I believe Avro is the right choice. Here is my reasoning against each criterion. I want you to disagree and improve it.' (4) Socialize before the review. Walk through the proposal with key stakeholders 1:1 before the group review. (5) Run the review, capture the decision, document the rationale.

The ADR (Architecture Decision Record) pattern: Every significant technical decision should be documented as an ADR. Format: Title, Status (proposed/accepted/deprecated), Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered. The ADR becomes the single source of truth for 'why did we do it this way?' — a question that every new team member asks and every postmortem needs to answer.

sections.codeBlock:
# Architecture Decision Record Template

# ADR-0017: Payment Service Event Format

## Status
- Proposed → Accepted (2026-03-15)

## Context
The payment service publishes events for order status changes
(created, confirmed, failed, refunded). These events are consumed
by 5 downstream services. We need a serialization format that
supports schema evolution (fields will be added over time).

## Decision
Use Apache Avro with Schema Registry. Rationale:
- Schema evolution: Avro supports default values for new fields
- Tooling: Kafka + Avro integration is mature at our company
- Performance: Avro binary encoding is 3x faster than JSON
- Client libraries: All 5 consuming services have Avro support

## Consequences
- All services must adopt schema registry
- Schema review becomes part of the design review process
- JSON debug output requires schema to decode (minor dev pain)

## Alternatives Considered
- Protocol Buffers: Stronger typing but heavier integration
  Rejected: gRPC pushback from 2 consuming services
- JSON Schema: No binary encoding, schema evolution is manual
  Rejected: Performance requirements (10K events/sec at P99 <100ms)
- MessagePack: No schema enforcement
  Rejected: Need for strict schema governance

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our team is too small for formal design reviews. We're 3 engineers moving fast. A weekly review slot would slow us down."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Design reviews are essential for any team regardless of size." (dismissive, doesn't address the specific concern about velocity)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Let's do a lightweight version. Two changes: (1) Require a 1-page design doc before any change that affects the data model or adds a new service. The doc template is 4 sections: Context, Proposal, Tradeoffs, Questions. Should take 30 minutes to write. (2) Do async review instead of a meeting — post the doc in Slack with a 24-hour review window. The reviewer responds inline. If there's disagreement after 24 hours, we escalate to a 15-minute sync. This catches architecture mistakes early (which saves velocity) without a fixed meeting slot. Watch for the pattern: if async reviews keep escalating, the team has outgrown async — then we add the weekly slot."
adversarialResponse.pattern: start-lightweight — offer the minimum viable process that still catches architectural mistakes. Scale up only when async review fails. The key is making design review feel like a velocity enabler, not a velocity blocker.

sections.title: Unblocking the Team at Scale

description.value:
The TL's primary operational responsibility is removing blockers. At FAANG scale, this is not about answering a question for an engineer — it is about building systems that reduce blocking events across the entire team.

Types of blockers and TL interventions: (1) Technical blockers — an engineer lacks information or skills to proceed. TL intervention: pair programming session, code review with detailed explanation, pointing to existing patterns in the codebase. (2) Dependency blockers — the team is waiting on another team. TL intervention: reach out to the other team's TL, negotiate timeline, find a workaround, escalate if needed. (3) Decision blockers — a design decision is stalled. TL intervention: drive the decision using the ADR process, break ties when senior engineers disagree. (4) Process blockers — CI/CD pipeline is slow, testing takes too long. TL intervention: prioritize infrastructure improvements, allocate 20% time to fixing the pipeline. (5) Organizational blockers — another team deprioritized your dependency. TL intervention: negotiate with the other EM or TL, escalate to your EM if needed, find alternative path.

The review queue discipline: A TL with 10+ pending code reviews is a bottleneck. Set a max queue size: 'I will never have more than 5 pending reviews.' When the queue hits 5, stop writing code and clear reviews. Use the 'review in 24 hours or auto-approve to secondary reviewer' rule for non-critical changes. For critical changes, review within 4 hours. Make your review expectations explicit in the team's working agreement: 'TL commits to reviewing within 24 hours. If urgent, @mention in Slack with [URGENT REVIEW].'

The 'blocked documentation' pattern: Every time you unblock an engineer, write down the answer. Not a full page — a paragraph in the team's FAQ doc or a comment in the relevant code. The first time an engineer asks 'how do I set up the local Kafka environment?' you show them. The second time, you point to the doc you wrote after the first time. The third time, the doc is self-serve and you never answer the question again. Over 6 months, this builds a documentation system that unblocks the team without your direct involvement.

items.label: Each engineer on team has had a 1:1 check-in (5 min) this week
Design decision log updated with any decisions made this week
Cross-team dependencies tracked and none are red (blocked)
One piece of documentation written or updated from this week's unblocking
At least one engineer unblocked via pairing, not just via answer-giving
Review queue distribution checked — are reviews balanced or is TL the bottleneck?

sections.title: The TL-PM Partnership

description.value:
The TL-PM relationship is one of the most critical partnerships at FAANG. At SAP, architects typically received functional specs from business analysts and translated them into technical designs. At FAANG, the TL and PM co-create the product strategy — the PM owns the 'what' and 'why,' the TL owns the 'how' and 'when.' Getting this partnership wrong is the fastest way to build the wrong thing, miss deadlines, or alienate the team.

The weekly TL-PM sync (30 min): Every Monday, 30 minutes. (1) Review current sprint progress — are we on track? (2) Review upcoming asks — what is the PM's latest request from leadership? (3) Flag risks — what tradeoffs does the PM need to be aware of? (4) Align on messaging — what story are we telling leadership this week? This sync replaces the need for ad-hoc decisions throughout the week.

Saying 'no' to a PM effectively: FAANG PMs are pushy by design. They are incentivized to ship features. The TL's job is to push back when the technical costs outweigh the product value. The pattern: 'I understand why you want X. Here is the technical cost: implementing X will require migrating the event format, which takes 3 weeks and blocks Y and Z. If we delay X to next quarter, we can deliver Y and Z on time. If X is this quarter's priority, we need to descope Y or Z. Which tradeoff do you prefer?' This reframes 'no' as a tradeoff decision that the PM and TL make together.

The PRD-engineering spec ritual: When a PM writes a PRD (Product Requirements Document), the TL should write a companion engineering spec that adds: (1) Technical feasibility assessment — can we actually build this with our current architecture? (2) Implementation phases — what gets built first, what gets deferred? (3) Risk analysis — what could go wrong and how do we mitigate? (4) Measurement plan — how will we know if this works? The TL publishes this spec within 1 week of the PRD. If the PRD and engineering spec conflict, the TL and PM resolve before the sprint planning.

SAP Bridge: 'At SAP, I worked with functional consultants who wrote functional specs. I would review the spec for technical feasibility, estimate effort, and identify risks. The translation to FAANG: functional specs become PRDs, functional consultants become PMs. The skill of reading a requirement and identifying the technical risks, edge cases, and architectural implications is the same — but at FAANG, you are expected to proactively shape the requirements, not just react to them.'

sections.codeBlock:
# TL-PM Weekly Sync Template

## Agenda (30 min, every Monday)

### 1. Sprint Health (5 min)
- Are we on track for this sprint's commitments?
- Any new blockers since Friday?
- Velocity trend: green/yellow/red

### 2. Incoming Asks (10 min)
- PM: What new requests came from leadership?
- TL: Technical assessment of each ask
- Decision: Accept / Defer / Push back

### 3. Risk Register (10 min)
- Top 3 technical risks this week
- TL proposes mitigations for each
- PM provides product perspective on tradeoffs

### 4. Leadership Messaging (5 min)
- TL: What technical progress do we highlight?
- PM: What product narrative are we telling?
- Align on the 3-sentence update for weekly review

### Outcomes
- Updated sprint backlog (if needed)
- Updated risk register
- Aligned leadership message

sections.title: Cross-Team Coordination and Dependency Management

description.value:
At the Staff+ TL level, your team's deliveries depend on multiple other teams — and their deliveries depend on you. Managing these dependencies without formal authority is the core coordination skill.

The dependency tracking system: (1) List every external dependency for each project — API contracts, data pipeline handoffs, library upgrades, deployment coordination. (2) For each dependency, identify: which team owns it, the commitment date, the criticality (blocking vs non-blocking), and the escalation path. (3) Track dependency health weekly — green (on track), yellow (at risk), red (blocked). (4) For red dependencies, escalate immediately — do not wait for the weekly sync. The pattern: 'Team A, our project is blocked on your API migration. Is there a way to ship the current API version first and migrate after our launch?'

The API contract as the coordination primitive: At FAANG, teams coordinate through API contracts, not meetings. The TL defines the interface between systems: 'Your service calls my service with this request format. My service returns this response. Here is the SLA. Here is the error handling contract.' The API contract is documented, versioned, and tested. If the contract changes, all consuming services are notified through the change management process. This replaces the SAP pattern of 'let's have a meeting to discuss the interface' with a scalable asynchronous coordination mechanism.

The TL coordination meeting (weekly, 60 min): A cross-team sync of TLs who share dependencies. Each TL shares: (1) What my team shipped this week that affects others. (2) What my team needs from others in the next 2 weeks. (3) What is at risk. The goal is not status reporting — it is early warning of cross-team issues. If TL A needs TL B's team to complete their database migration before TL A can start the event migration, and TL B's migration is slipping, TL A needs to know now, not at the quarterly review.

The 'push vs pull' dependency model: In a push model, the producing team pushes changes to consuming teams — they upgrade, they adapt. This puts the burden on consumers. In a pull model, consuming teams pull changes when they are ready — the producer maintains backward compatibility. FAANG TLs prefer the pull model because it reduces coordination overhead: 'We will maintain the old API for 6 months after the new one is available. Your team can upgrade when it fits your roadmap.' This costs the producing team more (maintaining backward compatibility) but reduces the coordination tax across the organization.

sections.codeBlock:
# Dependency Tracking Template

PROJECT: Payment Reconciliation Service

| Dependency | Owner Team  | Due Date | Status | Escalation      |
|------------|-------------|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Kafka 5.0 upgrade | Infra | 2026-04-15 | 🟢 | Contact: infr-tl |
| Payment API v2 contract | Payments | 2026-04-01 | 🟡 | Contact: pay-tl |
| Audit log schema change | Audit | 2026-03-20 | 🔴 | Escalated to EM |
| Cache cluster resize | Infra | 2026-04-30 | 🟢 | Contact: infr-tl |

# Weekly TL coordination update format
## (Send in Slack channel #tl-coordination every Tuesday)

**Shipped this week that affects others:**
- Deployed new event schema for payment-confirmed events
- No breaking changes — backward compatible

**Need from others (next 2 weeks):**
- Audit team: Need audit log schema change by Mar 20 (currently RED)
- Infra: Kafka 5.0 upgrade is on track for Apr 15

**Risks:**
- If audit log schema change slips past Mar 25, payment reconciliation launch shifts to May

sections.title: Growing the Next Generation of Tech Leads

description.value:
A Staff+ TL's impact is measured not just by what they deliver, but by how many other engineers they grow into TLs. The FAANG principle: 'Your legacy is the leaders you develop.' Growing a TL pipeline is how you demonstrate L7/Principal-level impact.

Identifying TL potential: Look for engineers who naturally: (1) Unblock others — when someone is stuck, do they help? (2) Ask clarifying questions — do they push back on vague requirements? (3) Think about the system — do they consider how their change affects downstream consumers? (4) Write things down — do they document decisions and share them? (5) Facilitate discussions — do they help resolve disagreements in design reviews?

The TL shadowing pattern: Pick one senior IC per quarter to shadow you as a TL. They attend all your TL meetings (design review, TL-PM sync, TL coordination) with you. Before each meeting, you brief them: 'Here is the context. Here is what I plan to achieve. Watch how I handle it.' After each meeting, you debrief: 'What did you notice? What would you have done differently? Why did I make the choices I made?' After 4-6 weeks of shadowing, they start leading one meeting per week with you in the room as backup. After 8-12 weeks, they lead a full project as TL with you as advisor.

The TL rotation: For teams with multiple senior engineers, rotate the TL role every 6-12 months. This (a) prevents TL burnout, (b) builds TL skills across more engineers, (c) brings fresh perspective to the team's technical direction, and (d) ensures the team is not dependent on a single TL. The outgoing TL stays as an advisor for the first month of the rotation, then steps back to focus on deep technical work or mentoring.

Measuring your TL multiplier: Track: (1) How many engineers on your team have led a cross-team initiative? (Target: 2+ per year.) (2) How many have shadowed you as TL? (Target: 3+ per year.) (3) How many TL rotation transitions have happened on your team? (Target: 1 per 6-12 months.) (4) What is the team's 'TL readiness' score — how many engineers could step into the TL role tomorrow if you left? Your promotion to L7/Principal depends on the answer to that last question.

sections.codeBlock:
# TL Shadowing Program Structure

## Month 1: Observe
- Attend all TL meetings with the shadow
- 15-min pre-brief before each meeting
- 15-min debrief after each meeting
- Shadow writes reflection: "What did the TL do that I wouldn't have?"

## Month 2: Co-lead
- Shadow leads 1 meeting per week (TL-PM sync first, easiest)
- You attend as backup, silent unless needed
- Debrief after each co-led meeting

## Month 3: Lead with advisor
- Shadow takes full TL responsibility for one project
- You are available for escalation but not proactive
- Weekly 30-min check-in: "What's going well? What's hard?"

## After Month 3: Independent
- Shadow is the designated TL for the next project
- You are the mentor, not the backup
- Quarterly check-in on TL growth areas

adversarialResponse.challenge: "I don't have time to grow other TLs. I'm already stretched thin as the only TL on a critical team. If I train someone else, they'll leave for another team."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Growing TLs is part of your job. Make time." (dismissive of the real concern about retention)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Two things: First, if you're the only TL, you have a bus-factor problem that's a risk to the entire org. Your leadership knows this and wants it fixed. Frame TL development as risk mitigation, not a nice-to-have. Second, the retention concern is real — but consider the alternative. If you don't grow TLs, you stay stuck as the TL forever, burning out. If you grow TLs, some will leave, but some will stay, and the ones who stay reduce your load. Over 12 months, even if 50% leave, the 50% who stay give you space to work on higher-impact problems. And if someone leaves because they became a TL under you? That's proof of your leadership. FAANG values TL multipliers — your reputation grows with every TL you develop, even if they join another team."
adversarialResponse.pattern: bus-factor-risk + retention-framing — reframe TL growth from personal cost to organizational risk reduction. Address the retention fear directly with numbers and reframe it as reputation-building.

sections.title: The SAP Engineer's Path to Tech Lead

description.value:
Most SAP engineers joining FAANG start as individual contributors (IC). The path to Tech Lead requires a deliberate transition from 'I produce technical output' to 'I enable my team to produce technical output.' This transition is harder for SAP engineers than for engineers who grew up in FAANG culture, because SAP's hierarchy and role definitions are more rigid.

Phase 1: Establish technical credibility (months 1-6 at FAANG): You cannot lead a team that doesn't trust your technical judgment. In the first 6 months: (1) Learn the system end-to-end. Read every design doc. Understand every service. Ask 'why was this built this way?' (2) Ship code. Make visible contributions. Fix a performance bug. Add monitoring. Refactor a painful code path. (3) Write a design doc. Propose a small architectural improvement. Get it reviewed and accepted. (4) Review code thoroughly. Become the reviewer that people want on their changes because your feedback is detailed and constructive. At the end of phase 1, the team thinks: 'When [you] reviews my code, I learn something.'

Phase 2: Take on coordination (months 6-12): (1) Volunteer for the cross-team dependency tracking role. Create the dependency spreadsheet. Send the weekly updates. (2) Facilitate a design review. Not the decision-maker — the facilitator who keeps the discussion on track and captures the outcome. (3) Run the team retro. (4) Write the engineering spec for the PM's PRD. At the end of phase 2, the team thinks: '[You] makes things happen across teams.'

Phase 3: Formal TL transition (months 12-18): (1) Discuss with your EM: 'I want to develop into the TL role. Can we create a plan?' (2) Propose a TL shadowing arrangement with the current TL or take TL responsibility for one project. (3) Define success criteria with your EM: 'I will know I'm ready for the TL role when: the team's review queue never exceeds 48 hours, the dependency tracker is current, and I've facilitated 4 design reviews.' (4) After the project succeeds, formalize the TL role at the next review cycle.

SAP-specific advantages: SAP engineers have strengths that FAANG TLs need: (1) System-level thinking — SAP architects understand complex, interconnected systems with 30+ year codebases. FAANG systems are younger but similarly complex. (2) Stakeholder management — SAP consultants negotiate with C-level executives, business analysts, and developers. FAANG TLs do the same with PMs, EMs, and other TLs. (3) Design documentation — SAP architects write detailed functional and technical specs. FAANG TLs write design docs, RFCs, and ADRs. The format is different but the discipline is the same. (4) Long-term thinking — SAP projects run for years, requiring architectural decisions that survive multiple releases. FAANG values this perspective — most FAANG engineers optimize for the next quarter, not the next 5 years.

The FAANG TL is not the SAP 'Lead Architect': At SAP, the Lead Architect often has authority — they define the architecture and others implement it. At FAANG, the TL has influence, not authority. The difference is critical: if you try to be a 'Lead Architect' at FAANG who tells other engineers what to do, you will fail. FAANG TLs persuade, facilitate, and enable — they do not command. The transition from 'I define the architecture' to 'I help the team define the architecture together' is the hardest mindset shift for SAP engineers on the TL path.

sections.codeBlock:
# SAP Engineer's 18-Month TL Path at FAANG

## Phase 1 (Months 1-6): Technical Credibility
- [ ] Read all team design docs (deep dives into 2-3 key systems)
- [ ] Ship 5+ meaningful code changes (not just trivial bug fixes)
- [ ] Write and get accepted 1 design doc
- [ ] Become go-to reviewer for 1 subsystem
- [ ] Goal: Team trusts your technical judgment

## Phase 2 (Months 6-12): Coordination Skills
- [ ] Own cross-team dependency tracking for 1 project
- [ ] Facilitate 3+ design review meetings
- [ ] Run 2+ team retrospectives
- [ ] Write engineering specs for 2+ PM PRDs
- [ ] Goal: Team sees you as an organizer, not just a coder

## Phase 3 (Months 12-18): TL Transition
- [ ] Have TL career conversation with EM
- [ ] Shadow current TL for 4-6 weeks
- [ ] Lead TL for 1 project (end-to-end)
- [ ] Formal TL role discussion at performance review
- [ ] Goal: Formal TL title or equivalent responsibility

checklist.prompt: Are You Ready for the TL Role? Self-Assessment

items.label: I can explain the team's system architecture end-to-end to a new hire
I am the go-to person for at least one part of the system
I regularly unblock other engineers without being asked
I have written 2+ design docs that were accepted by the team
I facilitate rather than dictate in design discussions
I prioritize team velocity over personal code output
My EM has discussed TL growth with me

details: FAANG Interview Tip: The TL role appears in Staff+ behavioral questions like 'Tell me about a time you set technical direction for a team' or 'How do you handle disagreement on architecture decisions?' Your answer should demonstrate: (1) You understand the difference between being the smartest engineer and being the TL. (2) You use processes (design reviews, ADRs, dependency tracking) to scale your decision-making, not just your personal judgment. (3) You have experience growing other engineers into TLs. If you were a 'team lead' or 'module owner' at SAP, frame that as TL experience: 'I was responsible for the technical direction of the pricing module across 3 teams. I ran design reviews, tracked dependencies with the finance team, and mentored 2 senior developers who later led their own modules.'
